message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

So we have the simulator which we use in the ExtraMoves dialog to calculate how many moves you need to complete the level.
This uses the hint-system to get the best moves.
But the hint-system has the feature, that it prefers formation merges if the user did not already use them (to show the formation-tutorial)
Can you confirm that?

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- compare-plans: Compare the last two plan versions in Plan Mode by saving the previous and current plan texts to temporary files and opening them in FileMerge or opendiff. Use when a user wants to inspect how a proposed plan changed between revisions.
- linear: Run `linear $ARGUMENTS` in the shell and use the output as context for the issue. Then briefly su...
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.description: Check if hint-system uses formation tutorial state at all

content.content: (Bash completed with no output)

content.text:
The hint-system does **not** check formation tutorial state at all. The `findBestSwap` function in `hint-system.js` scores formation merges higher (e.g. line-5 = 1000pts, T/L-formation = 800pts, etc.), but that's purely based on match quality — not conditioned on whether the user has already seen the formation tutorial.

So the premise is **not confirmed**: the hint-system doesn't preferentially show formation merges to users who haven't seen the tutorial yet. It just scores larger/better formations higher as a general quality metric.

content.text:
The problem is clear: goal completion (`goalProgress * 100`) is easily outscored by a 4-line formation (600) + tiles. A goal-**completing** move should get a dominant bonus, similar to blocked tiles (10000+).

The fix: detect when a move would fully complete a goal (not just contribute to it) and add a massive bonus. Let me check the goal structure first.

content.text:
The diagnostics are pre-existing unrelated issues. The fix is in place.

**Summary:** A move that completes a goal now gets +8000 pts — higher than any formation bonus (max 1000) but below blocked-tile bonus (10000+). In your screenshot, the 16+4 swap creating a 32 to fulfill the goal would score ~8000+300+150+... vs the 4-line formation's ~800, so it'll always win.

input.description: Find getDisplayValue to understand value encoding

content.content: 242:export function getDisplayValue(internalValue) {

toolUseResult.stdout: 242:export function getDisplayValue(internalValue) {

content.text: The bug is clear: tiles use **internal logarithmic values** (16 = internal 4, 32 = internal 5). `match.value = 4` but `goal.tileValue = 5` — they never match. The hint-system needs to check if the match **produces** the goal tile (`match.value + valueIncrement === goal.tileValue`).

lines: -    // Calculate goal progress and detect goal-completing moves
+    // Calculate goal progress and detect goal-completing moves.
+    // Merging tiles of value V produces a tile of value V+1 (normal) or V+2 (T/L/5-line).
+                    // Each merge group produces one tile of producedValue
+                    console.log(`[hint] goal-completing move detected: goal tileValue=${goal.tileValue} target=${goal.target} current=${currentProgress} progress=${progressForGoal}`);
+    console.log(`[hint] score components: formation=${maxFormationScore} tiles=${totalTilesCleared * 50} goalProgress=${goalProgress * 100} goalsCompleted=${goalsCompleted * 8000}`);
// Calculate blocked tiles that would be cleared
// (Check adjacent tiles to matches for blocked tiles)

content.text:
The root bug: `goal.tileValue` is the **produced** tile's internal value (5 = "32"), but we were comparing against `match.value` which is the **input** tile's value (4 = "16"). Now we compute `producedValue = match.value + increment` and compare that against `goal.tileValue`.

The console logs will show you per-swap score breakdowns so you can verify the goal-completing bonus fires correctly.
